data.title: Wall Street Lunch: NFL Eyes Bigger TV Bucks - Soonest
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

NFL Commissioner says the league is leaving money on the table. (0:15) New home sales with surprise surge in August. (2:20) Meet the autonomous commercial trucking disruptor. (2:57)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell says the league could begin renegotiating terms for its television rights with media partners as soon as 2026, four years earlier than the current agreement’s opt-out clause.

Goodell said on CNBC the NFL is leaving money on the table when you look at other recent sports media deals.

To revisit terms, the league would need to convince its current media partners Disney (NYSE:DIS), NBCUniversal (NASDAQ:CMCSA), Paramount (NASDAQ:PSKY), Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Fox (NASDAQ:FOX) (FOXA) to start discussions on any new deal.

Back in 2021, the NFL entered into an $111 billion, 11-year rights deal that includes an opt-out clause after the 2029-30 season with all of its media partners except Disney, which has one extra year of rights.

"I think our partners would want to sit down and talk to us at any time, and we continue to dialogue with them. I like that opportunity," Goodell said. "Obviously it’s not going to happen this year. But it could happen as early as next year. That could happen."

"The reason why we felt so strongly about the option is the landscape is changing. It could be a long-term deal with the benefit of having that stability and security of it. But I think the reality of it is it changes so quickly that you want to have the ability to move. I think those options are going to give us a lot of flexibility to potentially go earlier," he said.

If the NFL manages to secure a new deal, it could also hike the salary cap in future seasons, giving teams more money to spend on players and expand rosters.

Among active stocks, Micron (MU) is lower following a sell-the-news reaction to better-than-expected quarterly results and guidance.

Morgan Stanley analyst Joseph Moore, who has an Equal-Weight rating on Micron and kept his $160 price target, said he believes the stock is “expensive” on book value, “extremely expensive” on free cash flow metrics, and “expensive” on cycle adjusted earnings.

Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) is slumping after guiding for Q3 consolidated sales to come in lower by 4% for copper and 6% for gold compared with its July forecast.

The company previously forecast consolidated sales of 1 billion lbs of copper and 350,000 oz of gold for the quarter.

And Acadia Healthcare (ACHC) is rallying after activist Engine Capital reported a 3% stake in the behavioral healthcare operator and is pushing for board changes and other moves.

Engine Capital is urging Acadia to halt capital-intensive growth projects, fix its core business and review its asset portfolio.

On the economic front, August new home sales jumped 20.5% to an annual rate of 800,000 from 664,000 in July. That was well ahead of the 650,000 consensus and up 15.4% from a year ago.

In other news of note, autonomous trucking startup firm Gatik continues to emerge as one of the leading companies in the self-driving truck space.

The company announced a significant expansion of its commercial partnership with Loblaw Companies (OTCPK:LBLCF) in a development that it said marks a milestone for the autonomous trucking industry.

Loblaw will deploy Gatik's autonomous fleet across its distribution network in the Greater Toronto Area. Under the initial phase of the expansion, 20 autonomous trucks equipped with Gatik's next-generation sensor suite will be deployed by the end of 2025, with a further 30 to be deployed by the end of 2026, creating a scalable platform for future growth phases. The autonomous commercial truck fleet could be one of the largest in North America.

The expanded fleet of Isuzu-built trucks will initially be deployed with safety drivers onboard before transitioning to freight-only (driverless) operations, delivering goods with greater frequency and responsiveness to more than 300 Loblaw stores.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman Sachs analysts see potential GDP growth accelerating over the next few years to above 2% -- thanks to AI.

They estimate that AI will drive productivity growth to 1.7% through the end of 2029, and then 1.9% in the early 2030s. This would lead to potential GDP growth in the 2.1-2.3% range.

Earlier this year, Goldman estimated that generative AI could boost global GDP by 7% or about $7 trillion, lifting productivity growth by 1.5 percentage points over the next 10 years.

Analysts have also estimated that AI has added $160 billion to what they deem as “true GDP,” which includes enterprise software adoption, productivity gains, and capex toward AI infrastructure, as well as private-sector AI investments and productivity boosts that are often excluded from traditional metrics.

data.title: Navitas Semiconductor: Impressive Design Wins Overly Priced In - Limited Return Prospects
data.description:
NVTS' Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious - Monetization Likely Delayed, Pending Conversion To Purchase Orders & Confirmed NVDA Partnership

We previously covered Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:NVTS) in May 2025, discussing how its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) had offered great long-term growth potential, albeit with minimal near-term financial impact, since Rubin Ultra would only be launched from 2027 onwards.

Despite the growing new lifetime design wins and pipeline, we had believed that its ongoing cash burn and deteriorating balance sheet were likely to trigger further dilutive capital raises, worsened by the risks arising from the drastically higher short interest volume and the overly expensive valuations.

NVTS 1Y Stock Price

Since that initial Hold rating, the bulls have proven our bearish thesis wrong indeed, with NVTS further rallying by +57.8% while well outperforming the wider market at +14.3% and its silicon-based power semiconductor peers in varying degrees.

Much of its tailwinds are naturally attributed to the exuberant sentiments surrounding the multi-year cloud super cycle, as numerous market leaders across the foundry, infrastructure, SaaS, REIT, and energy end markets highlight the durability of AI-related spending trends.

While some of the optimism appears to be warranted, as NVDA, the AI accelerator market leader, continues to guide double-digit FQ3 2026 (CQ3 2025) revenue growth, we believe that NVTS' rally has been overly done indeed.

This is because NVTS has been and may continue to report mixed sales cadence in the near term, attributed to the softer demand in the solar, industrial, and EV end markets, which consequently contributed to the impacted FQ2 2025 revenues of $14.49M (+3.4% QoQ/-29.1% YoY).

The management has already highlighted further sequential industrial weakness over the next few quarters as well, which perhaps explains the underwhelming FQ3 2025 revenue guidance of $10M (-30.9% QoQ/-53.9% YoY) and adj. gross margin guidance of 38.5% (in line QoQ/-1.2 YoY):

And then we've not seen ramp-ups of new design wins given the industry slowdowns continuing, I think, for a couple of quarters more. It's a little hard to predict even by our larger competitors. So I'd kind of give them equal weight on driving some of the sequential decline in Q3. (Seeking Alpha)

At the same time, the ongoing tariff headwinds are likely to be painful, since China (including Hong Kong) comprises 72% of NVTS' overall revenues in FQ2 2025 (-2 points YoY), worsened by the ongoing decline in the country's industrial/ manufacturing activities - with it likely to contribute to its lower sales trends ahead.

This is on top of the narrowing of its product portfolio as the management opts to transition away from the lower margin mobile products to data center-related/Gallium nitride/Silicon carbide technologies - with it underscoring why the recent stock price rally has not made sense, since the ongoing R&D efforts and the future commercialization cadence are likely to be a multi-year process.

Moving forward, despite the prior optimism surrounding the NVDA development collaboration, NVTS investors may want to temper their expectations indeed.

Our caution arises from the fact that NVDA has extended the collaboration invitation to NVTS' direct analog/SiC competitors, including ON Semiconductor Corporation (ON), STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM), Renesas Electronics Corporation (OTCPK:RNECF), and Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), with it uncertain which company may eventually emerge as the winner.

The same has been highlighted by NVTS in the recent earnings call, based on the ongoing evaluation by Q4 2025, the final supplier selection/system design completion by 2026, and the volume production only expected in 2027 - with it exemplifying the highly speculative and competitive nature of the ongoing engagements with NVDA.

At the same time, we must highlight that out of NVTS' $2.4B of supposed pipeline (estimated potential future business), only $450M of design wins (selection of a Navitas product for a specific production program, stated in terms of revenues that may be realized over the life of the customer's end product) have been confirmed as of last quarter.

Given many of these are still in the "engineering sample" stages, it remains to be seen when these design wins may actually be realized as revenues, pending customer evaluation, purchase orders, mass production, and delivery.

Therefore, while NVTS may have previously highlighted 75 wins in data centers, 40 wins in EV e-mobility, 28 wins in appliance industrial, 100 wins in solar/energy storage, and 180 wins in mobile consumer in the FQ1'25 earnings call, we urge investors to closely monitor their announcements over the next few quarters, pending further clarification.

Otherwise, we believe that NVTS has already made the prudent choice to migrate some of its customers to its alternative foundry partner, Powerchip, attributed to the improved cost and capacity advantages, while potentially triggering lower prices and higher volumes.

Assuming that much of those design wins are converted into "purchase orders," we may see the company report a robust FY 2026 performance as well, before its data center monetization kicks in from FY 2026 onwards.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These developments perhaps explain why the consensus forward estimates remain mixed, with NVTS not expected to report meaningful top-line growth and breakeven performance in the near term.

The silver lining to NVTS' investment thesis will be the management's opportunistic capital raise at current inflated stock prices, as observed in the richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $161.18M (+114.5% QoQ/ +43.9% YoY).

This may allow the yet profitable company to bridge the ongoing cash burn at an annualized rate of -$42.24M (+10.6% QoQ/+20.6% YoY), albeit with further equity dilution as observed in the higher share count at 198.95M (+5.9% QoQ/+8.6% YoY).

NVTS Valuations

Given the uncertain top-line growth prospects at a consensus 3Y CAGR estimates of +4.5%, it goes without saying that NVTS is rather lofty at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 27.55x.

This is compared to the 1Y mean of 5.22x (prior to the NVDA announcement), the sector median of 3.50x, and its silicon-based power semiconductor peers, including:

  • ON at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 3.64x and a 3Y projected top-line growth at a CAGR of -0.3%,
  • STM at 1.91x/+3.8%,
  • RNECF at 3.39x/+5.1%,
  • Infineon Technologies AG (OTCQX:IFNNY) at 3.21x/+6.3%,
  • TXN at 9.73x/+10.9%,
  • Power Integrations, Inc. (POWI) at 4.69x/+13.4%, and
  • Wolfspeed, Inc. (WOLF) at 7.04x/+14.8%, respectively,

with it apparent that NVTS' uncertain growth prospects from the highly competitive NVDA collaboration and the inflated valuations do not offer a margin of safety here.

If anything, based on the rather elevated Enterprise Value of $1.37B by the time of writing (up from pre-NVDA partnership levels of $298.10M) and the consensus downgraded FY2027 revenue estimates from $154.49M at the start of the year to $94.95M by the time of writing, it is unsurprising that NVTS remains extremely expensive at our estimated FY 2027 EV/Sales valuations of 14.42x, with it underscoring its mixed return prospects at current heights.

Lastly, readers must also note the risks tied to single-client concentration if the NVDA partnership eventually works out, with it likely to trigger headwinds to NVTS' prospects if the former so chooses to diversify their supply sources and/or the AI bubble bursts before monetization kicks in by 2027.

So, Is NVTS Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

NVTS 4Y Stock Price

For now, NVTS has already regained part of its recent losses through the +29.2% stock price recovery from the September 2025 bottom, thanks to the overly done AI hype post Q2'25 earnings season, with the company's mixed fundamentals and the overly expensive valuations implying the stock's uncertain return prospects from current heights.

NVTS' Overbought Position

Thanks to the high double digits rally, it is unsurprising that NVTS is also overbought by the time of writing, with technical indicators and historical trends already implying a potential near-term correction, worsened by the drastically higher short interest volumes by +52.1% on a YoY basis.

Combined with the prolonged NVDA collaboration, the uncertain monetization of its design wins, and the potentially lumpy industrial demand recovery, we prefer to maintain our prior Hold rating here.

It may be more prudent to adopt a wait-and-see attitude in the meantime.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Core Plus Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The second quarter of 2025 was defined by several key policies from the new Trump administration, including tariffs/trade, immigration, and taxes. “Liberation Day” was marked by tariffs that were significantly higher than market expectations. This created a stretch of financial market volatility while economists and analysts recalibrated the potential economic implications. Market volatility was followed by policy implementation delays and legal challenges when the tariffs went into effect. Final tariff and tax policy outcomes remain unknown, and trade deals signed to date have been limited. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter when the economy seemed to remain solid and inflation continued to improve.

Despite the policy uncertainty, most U.S. data continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth was slowing, inflation was moderating, and unemployment remained low by historical standards. This allowed the Federal Reserve to maintain a patient easing bias while it awaited greater clarity on the impact of the new policies on the economy. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. This, along with fiscal developments in Europe and the U.S., had meaningful foreign exchange impacts and resulted in a weaker U.S. dollar during the period. Geopolitical developments were decidedly mixed during the second quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Middle East and little progress in the Russia/Ukraine conflict.

The weight of U.S. policy uncertainty lifted as the quarter progressed and resulted in more varied financial market performance. Fixed income sector performance was also variable, with interest rates mixed depending on tenor during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased 16 basis points (bps), the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 13 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield increased by 6 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 25 bps higher.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I)(MUTF:SAVYX) returned 1.57% in the second quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index return of 1.21%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Selection within the sector was positive.
  • High Yield Corporates: Allocation to the sector was positive.

Performance Detractors

  • U.S. Treasury Curve Positioning: While the underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact on performance, curve positioning was a detractor.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to agency mortgage-backed securities and IG corporates. The overall cash allocation was also higher.
  • Reduced exposure to bank loans and non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund’s sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

Emerging Market (EM) Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The return on the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index was favorable in 2Q at 3.29%, with EM high yield’s 4.28% outperforming EM IG’s 2.29% during the period. EM corporate debt returned 1.58%. During the period, Argentina was approved for a $20 billion IMF program, Ecuador re-elected Daniel Noboa, and Romania’s second-round presidential election saw the victory of centrist Nicușor Dan. Throughout the quarter, the overall EM exposure in the Fund was unchanged. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and EM high yield over EM IG.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads began 2Q at 94 bps, surged to 119 bps after Liberation Day tariffs were announced, and subsequently rallied all the way back into the mid-80s by quarter-end. This positions spreads within 10 bps of the 20-year lows reached in late 2024. Yields at 4.99% are slightly lower sequentially, driven by this decline in spread. Heavy outflows at the start of the quarter gave way to consistent inflows, albeit lower than 1Q levels. Gross supply of $375 billion in the quarter is in line with the five-year average (ex-2020). We favor U.S. banks as their fundamentals have improved. Within industrials, we have focused on defensive and domestic-oriented industries such as utilities and pipelines.

High Yield Corporates: The high yield market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 3.53% in 2Q. Credit spreads tightened 57 bps to +290 bps. Positive trade developments, prospects for rate cuts, easing geopolitical tensions, robust inflows at quarter-end, and supportive earnings contributed to the strong performance. Healthcare outperformed on prospects of more benign legislative treatment, while energy underperformed due to weaker oil prices.

Securitized: After the spread widening from Liberation Day tariff news, all markets settled down, and the securitized sector experienced some spread tightening as the quarter ended, with strong demand meeting a plethora of new issue volumes. This, coupled with lower U.S. Treasury rates, enabled securitized markets to post positive total and excess returns. Though our investable dollars mostly remain higher in the capital stack, we are selectively looking at individual transactions where we believe opportunities may exist lower in the capital stack.
